paragraphs.text: Being a relatively young person and growing up with social media and all the risks and predatory behaviour that comes with it (not just from other people online, but rather from the organizations like Instagram, Reddit, [...] themselves) I am genuinely glad to be living in the EU as it seems to be the ONLY place that constantly adjusts the law to protect us consumers. Having said that, there is still A LOT wrong with the way things on the internet work and the big companies handle their business and we as consumers are still to this day, even after all the new rules that have been implemented, always target of these predatory and misleading strategies. The most important and noteworthy are the predatory and "hidden" advertisements in between other social media content (similar to DFA Art.25 §10) Its happened not only a handful of times that I was scrolling through the likes of Instagram, Reddit or (*especially*) Pinterest and I clicked on what seemed like a normal Post only to be taken to some random Website instead of the "Detail-View" of the Post because I missed the laughably small "Advertisement" Banner in the bottom or top corner. Most of the time its even hidden in some way or another, either by reducing the Texts legibility by placing it on some background or changing its foreground color or by reducing the font size to "barely-readable-even-if-i'm-wearing-my-glasses". This is especially bad for the above mentioned platforms, as they also make an effort to make the advertisements to seem like normal content someone has posted (Instagram displaying them like any other Post except for that tiny banner, Reddit plainly putting Ads in between threads with almost no visual distinction between what is an Ad and what is an actual thread, Pinterest by just plainly placing Ad-Images on your boards that sometimes do NOT look the least like an Ad) and they all take you to some website where you can buy stuff without asking you, if you want to leave the app first. I also wanted to take a quick moment to address two more points that may or may not be out of scope but I still wanted to put them out there, as they don't seem right to me. As you might have heard (this has made quite the news all around the globe) Mastercard and Visa have recently "allegedly" pressured Steam into removing a ton of games they didn't like by threatening to pull their payment processing for the whole platform if they didn't comply. They claimed they were only asking to remove content that didn't comply with local law but that wasn't the case for most of the products removed. Same happened to Itch.io around the same time. In my humble opinion, nor Mastercard or Visa or ANY OTHER company for that matter should be able to have such a power and be allowed to blackmail other companies into censoring their inventory just because they feel like it. Lastly I want to speak out my support for the StopKillingGames Initiative, as I think it critical to stop game developers from being able to disable/break future games as they so often already did with older titles. Just to name two good examples here: "The Crew" by Ubisoft and "Deathgarden" by Behaviour. These and many other games before them were rendered unusable by the Developers by shutting down support for good. I DO ACKNOWLEDGE that servers cannot remain online forever as they are a running expense that somehow has to get paid. I get that. BUT rendering even a SINGLE PLAYER campaign, which should be OFFLINE, unusable after the discontinuance is not only a horrible move by the company but straight up criminal (in my opinion). I mean that is like me launching my own company selling clothes and someday deciding I want to end support for the line I sold a year ago, so I just visit everyone and take back what they had legally purchased from me. This is NOT OK. Not even if you are attaching a ridiculous "You do only get a license if you purchase this" disclaimer to checkout. Thanks for reading and have a great day!!
